name: Nehalem River gravels
short_description: Collect agates and jasper from productive river gravels on BLM land near the Nehalem River. No permit required, beginner-friendly, with 25 lbs daily limit.
description:
The Nehalem River gravels in Clatsop County offer excellent rockhounding opportunities on public BLM land where recreational collectors can search for agates, jasper, and chalcedony without permits. This location sits in the Cascade Range foothills where ancient Tertiary volcanic activity and subsequent river transport have concentrated semi-precious stones in accessible gravel bars and benches alongside the river.

The terrain is relatively flat at 414 feet elevation with gentle slopes, making this an ideal site for families and beginners. The Nehalem River valley has a long history of small-scale prospecting, and modern rockhounds still find excellent specimens in the loose gravels. The combination of active and old river deposits means productive material is often exposed after winter high-water events.

What to expect: colorful banded agates ranging from thumbnail to fist-sized, translucent chalcedony nodules, and patterned jasper pieces. The gravels are accessible along the riverbank and adjacent benches, allowing you to work multiple zones in a single outing. The flat terrain means you can cover ground easily without scrambling or climbing.

This is a no-hassle location - bring a collecting bag, spend a few hours working the gravels, and you can easily fill your 25-pound daily limit. The open BLM status and lack of fees make it a favorite for local collectors and visitors alike.
history: The Nehalem River gravels were formed during the Tertiary period when Cascade Range volcanic activity produced silica-rich deposits that were subsequently eroded and transported downriver by multiple glacial melt events and normal river action. Over millions of years, these processes concentrated agate, jasper, and chalcedony in the river gravels, where they remain accessible today.
tips: Focus your search on gravel bars along the river's edge and elevated benches above the current waterline, where heavier stones accumulate. Look for distinctive banded agate patterns and the glassy luster of chalcedony - these are easier to spot than dull rocks. A small classifier or screen speeds up material sorting in the gravels. Check the site after winter storms when high water has moved and exposed fresh material from upstream sources.
terrain_notes: Flat terrain at 414 feet elevation with gentle slopes. The Nehalem River valley features open gravel bars and vegetated banks typical of the Oregon Coast Range. Walking distance from parking to productive gravels is typically less than a quarter mile. Footing is uneven on river gravels, requiring sturdy shoes, but no climbing or scrambling needed.
written_directions: From Longview, Washington, head south on I-5 for 15 miles, then take the Kalama exit toward Portland. Follow US 30 southwest through Washington and into Oregon for approximately 40 miles. Continue on US 30 toward Astoria, watching for Nehalem River access points near milepost markers in the Clatsop County area. Turn onto local roads toward river access points - specific pullouts and BLM access roads branch north and south off US 30 near the river. GPS coordinates will guide you to productive gravel bars on public land.
road_conditions: US 30 is paved highway. Access roads to specific gravel bars may be unpaved but generally passable with standard vehicles. Some pullouts and access roads may be rough or narrow - high-clearance helpful but not required.
parking_notes: Informal pullouts along US 30 and accessing roads near river access points. No developed parking lot. Limited capacity - typically room for 2-4 vehicles per pullout.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA. No commercial collection. No motorized excavation. Collecting tools (pick, classifier, small shovel) are permitted for hand-operated use.
rules:
Personal use only
Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA
No commercial collection
No motorized excavation
No power tools or heavy equipment
Respect active mining claims if posted
nearest_services: Longview, Washington (35 miles northwest) has gas, restaurants, and supplies. Astoria, Oregon (25 miles southwest) offers smaller-town services.
accessibility_notes: Relatively flat terrain accessible to most hikers and families. Uneven gravel footing requires sturdy footwear but no climbing or scrambling. Riverside location means some areas may be muddy or wet. Not wheelchair accessible for most sections, but nearby observation is possible from parking areas.

faq.question: What types of agates and jasper can I find in the Nehalem River gravels?
faq.answer: The Nehalem River gravels produce Oregon's classic banded agates with distinctive red, white, and gray patterns, translucent chalcedony nodules, and patterned jasper pieces. Sizes typically range from thumbnail to fist-sized specimens. The variety of colors and patterns is excellent, with many find-worthy pieces easily visible in the loose gravels.

faq.question: Do I need a permit to collect rocks at Nehalem River on BLM land?
faq.answer: No permit is required for recreational rockhounding on this BLM land. You only need to follow the 25-pound daily limit per FLPMA and collect for personal use only. This makes it an easy, no-hassle location for beginner rockhounders.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to rockhound near Clatsop County?
faq.answer: May through September offers the most comfortable conditions in the Nehalem River area. Winter storms can expose fresh material, but rainy season brings high water levels that limit access. Early fall and late spring are ideal - mild weather, lower river levels, and freshly moved gravels from spring runoff.

faq.question: How much can I collect at Nehalem River gravels per visit?
faq.answer: You can collect up to 25 pounds per day on this BLM land under FLPMA regulations for personal use. Most rockhounders fill this limit within 2-3 hours of productive searching, making it efficient for day trips.

faq.question: Is Nehalem River rockhounding good for beginners and families?
faq.answer: Yes - the flat terrain, easy access, abundant material, and no permit requirement make this ideal for beginners and families. Kids age 6 and up can actively search and find specimens. The short walk from parking to productive gravels works well for younger collectors.

faq.question: What tools and gear do I need for rockhounding the Nehalem River gravels?
faq.answer: Bring a collecting bag or bucket, sturdy walking shoes, a small hand pick or classifier for sorting through gravels, and water. The loose gravels require minimal digging - most material is surface-visible. A magnifying loupe helps identify agate and chalcedony luster. Work gloves protect hands from sharp gravel edges.

meta_description: Find agates, jasper, and chalcedony in Clatsop County BLM gravels. No permit, 25 lb daily limit. Easy rockhounding near Longview, WA.
